summary:
The Sanchi Stupa (c. 250–200 BC) is the oldest surviving stone structure in India, built under [[event:1098|Ashoka the Great]]'s patronage as part of his program of [[event:1101|Spread of Buddhism under Ashoka]], and subsequently adorned with four elaborately carved gateways (toranas) containing the most comprehensive early narrative sculpture of the life of the Buddha.

Ashoka reportedly built 84,000 stupas across his empire; Sanchi is among the few that survive. The Great Stupa at Sanchi began as a simple hemispherical brick structure over the Buddha's relics, then was enlarged and encased in stone. The four gateways added by the [[event:1103|Shunga Dynasty]] (2nd–1st centuries BC) are covered with intricate carvings depicting Jataka stories (tales of the Buddha's previous lives), scenes from the Buddha's life, and events from Ashoka's reign — constituting an encyclopedic visual narrative without parallel in early Buddhist art. The absence of direct human representations of the Buddha (shown only as symbols — footprints, an empty throne, an umbrella) reflects early Buddhist artistic conventions before the [[event:1216|Gandhara Art Origins]] tradition introduced human Buddha images. Sanchi is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a living pilgrimage center.
